fact: December 14 is exactly one week before the winter solstice in the Northern Hemisphere, which means the sun is getting lazier and lazier each day!
In Antarctica, penguins are experiencing their longest days - the sun barely sets and they get to waddle around in nearly 24-hour daylight!
December 14 is peak evergreen season when pine and fir trees in northern climates pump out their strongest, most delicious scents to survive the cold!
Arctic foxes are typically showing off their fluffiest winter coats by this date - their fur is so thick it's like wearing 10 sweaters at once!
The Geminid meteor shower peaks around this date every year, turning the night sky into nature's own fireworks show with up to 120 shooting stars per hour!
In the Southern Hemisphere, December 14 falls in the middle of summer - it's swimming weather while the north is bundling up!
Reindeer in the Arctic are living in total darkness by this date because the sun hasn't risen in weeks - they navigate by moonlight and their super-powered noses!
December 14 falls deep in Sagittarius season, which means if the zodiac were a school year, we'd be in the 'adventure and exploration' unit right now!
Maple trees in cold climates have completely shut down their food-making by this date - they're basically taking a 4-month nap!
The sun sets so early in northern places like Alaska that by December 14, some towns only get 3-4 hours of daylight - like living in permanent twilight!
Hibernating animals like bears have typically been snoozing for weeks by now, and their heart rates have slowed down so much they only beat 8 times per minute!
December 14 marks the time when ice usually starts forming thick enough on northern lakes for the first brave ice fishers to drill holes and drop their lines!
